title: practical english usage - Part 131
content:
We understood none of his arguments.  I've been to none of those places.

She's done none of the work, (     .. . -no-efthe-werk.) Before a determiner (e.g. the, my, this) or a pronoun, we  use none of.

There weren't any messages ...) There were no messages for you this morning I'm afraid. (More emphatic than time.) Sorry I can't stop. I've got no time. (More emphatic than .../ haven't got any ... There isn't a mirror...) Would you believe it? There's no mirror in the bathroom! (More emphatic than negative idea.

No can be used instead of not ɑ  or not any when we  want to emphasise a 1  no:  emphatic 163 no,   none      and   not    a  /any For more information about any, ►     159.   For every, »151.

another and another) (     . . .-magazines of any kind.) On  the stand there were newspapers and magazines of every kind. (= one and not another or another) (     . . . Every one.) ‘ Which newspaper would you like? 'It doesn't matter. Any one.' (= one or to ‘ all’ . Compare: 'whichever one you choose'. Every looks at things together: its meaning is closer The  meaning is not quite the same. Any looks at things one at a time: it means Any/Every child can learn to swim.

class or group.

Any and every can both be used to talk in general about all the members of a 162 any     and   eve   ry:  the   difference For the  free choice' use of any (e.g. Any child could do this), ►     160.

parked in this road, they will. ..) Any cars parked in this road will be towed away. (= If there are any cars Sometimes any is used to suggest 'if there is/are any'.

If you want some/any help, let me know.

Both some and any are common in //-clauses.

The guards prevented us from asking any questions.

There is little point in doing any more work now.

There's hardly any tea left. We got there without any trouble.

You never give me any help.  I forgot to get any bread.

meaning: for example never, hardly, without, little.

We   use any in affirmative clauses after words that have a negative or limiting 3 any     in  affirmative        clauses grammar •  164   no one and none For more about none, ►     163.

'Have you found any blackberries?* 'Not one.* not Not one of my shirts is clean, (     No one of my shirts . . .) I haven't read any of his books.

None of my friends wished me a happy birthday.

not one (of) (more emphatic). No one is not used in this way.

To  express the idea 'not a single one (of)', we  can use none (of), not any (of) or 2  none I stayed in all evening waiting but no one came.

No one wished me a happy birthday, (     No-one-of my friends ...) It cannot be followed by of.

No one (also written no-one in British English) means the same as nobody.

ɪ64 no   one     and   none For no as a modifying adverb (e.g. no better), ►    373.

For no and not, ►    536.   For more about any, ►     159.   For none and no one, ►     164.

Nobody spoke, (     Net anybody spoke.) I saw nobody. (More emphatic than I didn't see anybody.) Nobody, nothing, no one and nowhere are used in similar ways to no.

6 nobody,        etc not Neither of my parents could be there, (     None of my parents-...) We   use neither, not no or none, to talk about two people or things (►   157).

5 not   used    to  talk   about     two not None of my friends lives near me. (     Not-any-of my friends-. ..) not No tourists ever came to our village, (     Not any tourists...) not No brand of cigarette is completely harmless, (     Not any brand-. ..) Not any cannot normally be used in subjects. No and none of are used instead.

A whale is not a fish, (     A whale-is-nefish - the sense is not emphatic.) He's no fool. (= He's not a fool at all. - emphatic negative) emphatic. Compare: We   prefer not a/any in objects and complements when the sense is not 3 not    a!any 'How many of the books have you read?1 'None.' We   can use none alone if the meaning is clear.

None of my friends are interested, (more informal) None of my friends is interested, (more formal) (a little more formal) or plural (a little more informal).

When  we  use none o/with a plural noun or pronoun, the verb can be singular Determiners:       Quantifiers        Section  13 grammar »165    much  and many clauses. ►    461, 517 Far and long (= a long time) are also used mostly in questions and negative economists, ...
